---
title: "Drill planning under uncertainty"
subtitle: "Two pieces, two scales — POMDP on a regional porphyry-Cu prior and a Gaussian process on a placer-claim grade surface"
date: 2026-05-12
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
---

## Setup

A prospectivity model gives you a per-cell probability that a deposit
exists. It does not tell you where to drill next given what you've
already drilled. Real exploration campaigns are sequential: each hole
costs money, returns information about the local geology, and that
information should update the next-hole decision.

This chapter collects the two pieces of decision-theoretic
drill-planning work in this portfolio, at very different scales:

1. **BCGT POMDP decision planning** — regional porphyry-Cu prospectivity
   prior (BC Golden Triangle, ~3,500 prior-positive cells over a
   ~90,000 km² AOI), framed as a partially observable Markov decision
   process and solved with Monte Carlo Tree Search (POMCP). Methodology
   port of *Intelligent Prospector* v1.0 (Mern et al., *Geosci. Model
   Dev.* 16:289, 2023). Three policies compared side by side: random,
   greedy, POMCP. See
   [BCGT POMDP decision planning](../bcgt/decision_planning.qmd).

2. **Bear Cub Gaussian-process drill-site recommender** — single-claim
   placer-gold property (Cape Nome MS 1178, 24 historical drill holes
   over a 597,000 ft² patent), framed as Bayesian regression on
   (x, y) → pay-zone grade with a per-location uncertainty estimate
   from the GP posterior variance. Two recommender criteria
   compared: maximum predictive variance (pure information gain) and
   expected improvement against a target grade. See
   [Bear Cub GP recommender](https://johnsondevco.com/bearcub/notebooks/bear_cub/gp_recommender.html).

The two pieces use different mathematical machinery (POMDP / MCTS
versus GP regression / acquisition functions) and target different
exploration questions (sequential drilling on a regional prior versus
next-hole picks on a single property), but they answer the same
class of question: *given what we know about the subsurface, where
should the next hole go?*

## Why these two together

The prospectivity-mapping work in chapters 2-4 produces probability
surfaces. A practical exploration team has a budget that converts
probability into hole-location decisions. The mathematical step from
probability surface to decision is what this chapter is about.

Two ways the bridge gets crossed in practice:

- **At regional scale**, the question is "given a per-cell
  prospectivity prior over thousands of square kilometers, where
  should we sequentially drill to maximize expected resource value
  while staying within a fixed budget?" The POMDP framing handles
  the sequential dependency directly: each hole's result updates
  the belief state, the planner re-solves, the next decision
  reflects everything learned so far. The BCGT notebook walks through
  POMCP (a sample-based MCTS planner) on the BCGT prior and
  benchmarks it against random and greedy baselines.

- **At property scale**, the question is "given a sparse grid of
  historical drill data, where should the next hole go to
  maximize information gain or expected grade?" The GP framing
  handles the sparse-data uncertainty directly: the posterior
  variance is highest where data is sparsest, and an acquisition
  function (max-variance, expected-improvement) chooses points to
  drill that optimize against either uncertainty or grade. The
  Bear Cub notebook applies this to 24 holes on a 597,000 ft²
  patent and outputs two recommended next-hole locations under
  different acquisition criteria.

## Limits

The POMDP work is a methodology demonstration on a regional prior,
not a production-grade resource model. The path-planning policy is
single-hypothesis (the prior is fixed, not updated mid-campaign),
which is the v1.0 simplification rather than the v2.0 multi-hypothesis
extension. Real exploration would also need to handle drill-cost
gradients (terrain, access), real per-hole information yield
distributions (not the simulated ones we use), and the practical
constraint that drill rigs cost money to move. The notebook documents
these limits explicitly.

The Bear Cub GP recommender produces a defensible next-hole pair
even with n=24 holes and a spatial-only GP that doesn't predict
pay-zone grade well in absolute terms (negative leave-one-out R²
because the beach-line geology creates a gradient that 24 samples
can't constrain). The recommender output is still meaningful
because the GP posterior variance is a faithful map of where the
data is sparse, even when the GP mean is unreliable.

## Files

- [BCGT POMDP decision planning](../bcgt/decision_planning.qmd) (full notebook)
- [Bear Cub GP recommender](https://johnsondevco.com/bearcub/notebooks/bear_cub/gp_recommender.html) (full notebook)
- [Bear Cub dark-data ingestion pipeline](https://johnsondevco.com/bearcub/notebooks/bear_cub/main.html) (parent notebook the GP recommender was extracted from)
- [BCGT regional pipeline](../bcgt/bcgt_porphyry_prospectivity.qmd) (the prior the POMDP runs on top of)

## Sources

- Mern, J. et al. 2023. *Intelligent Prospector v1.0: a robotic exploration planner for mineral prospectivity mapping.* *Geosci. Model Dev.* 16, 289-302.
- Mern, J. et al. 2024. Intelligent Prospector v2.0: scalable POMDP planning with information-gain rewards. arXiv:2410.10610.
- Rasmussen, C. E. and Williams, C. K. I. 2006. *Gaussian Processes for Machine Learning*. MIT Press. (Reference for GP regression + acquisition functions.)
